text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN
Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION:
38-year-old woman (now age 39) with metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma, germline BRCA1 mutation. Known sites of disease at last imaging: right 5th posterior rib (status post palliative radiation), T10 vertebral body, right iliac wing osseous lesions, and two hypoattenuating lesions in the hepatic right lobe. Currently on olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID monotherapy initiated approximately 3 months ago, along with zoledronic acid for bone metastases. Examination requested for restaging and treatment response assessment.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and whole-body bone scan performed approximately 3 months ago at time of metastatic disease confirmation.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following intravenous administration of 90 mL iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350) with portal venous phase imaging of the abdomen and pelvis. Slice thickness 5 mm axial reconstructions; 2.5 mm coronal and sagittal reformats generated. Whole-body planar bone scintigraphy performed following IV injection of approximately 25 mCi Tc-99m MDP; delayed imaging obtained at 3 hours post-injection, anterior and posterior projections from skull to feet.

---

CT CHEST WITH CONTRAST

Lungs and Airways:
The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidative opacities identified. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The visualized airways are patent without endobronchial lesions. Trachea and mainstem bronchi unremarkable.

Mediastinum and Hila:
No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Cardiomediastinal silhouette within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. Heart size normal.

Right Chest Wall / Rib:
Right 5th posterior rib: Previously identified lytic lesion with cortical disruption now demonstrates sclerotic rim surrounding the lytic component, measuring approximately 1.4 cm (previously 1.5 cm). This appearance is consistent with post-radiation healing response. Mild irregularity of the cortex remains. No acute fracture identified. No adjacent soft tissue mass.

Left Chest Wall:
No suspicious osseous lesions. Left breast: expected post-lumpectomy and post-radiation changes in the upper outer quadrant with architectural distortion, focal skin thickening, and surgical clip artifact. No new suspicious soft tissue mass.

Axillae:
No enlarged lymph nodes bilaterally.

---

CT ABDOMEN WITH CONTRAST

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and contour. Two previously described hypoattenuating lesions in the right hepatic lobe are identified:
  - Segment VII lesion: Previously 1.2 cm, now measuring 0.9 cm in greatest axial dimension. Persistently hypoattenuating with mild peripheral enhancement on portal venous phase. No central necrosis or hemorrhagic change.
  - Segment VI lesion: Previously 0.8 cm, now measuring 0.6 cm. Faint, hypovascular. No significant enhancement.
No new hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent.

Spleen:
Normal size and attenuation. No focal lesions.

Pancreas:
Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation or focal masses.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands without adenopathy or mass lesion.

Kidneys:
Bilateral kidneys enhance symmetrically without focal cortical masses or hydronephrosis. No perinephric fat stranding.

Gastrointestinal:
No bowel obstruction. No mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No ascites.

Vascular:
Aorta and major abdominal vessels are patent without aneurysmal change.

---

CT PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

Right Iliac Wing:
Previously described lytic lesion with cortical disruption in the right iliac wing, measuring approximately 1.8 cm at prior examination. On current study, the lesion measures 1.7 cm with minimal interval change. The cortical disruption is stable. No new adjacent soft tissue component. No new acetabular involvement. The opposite (left) iliac wing is unremarkable.

Lymphatics:
No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Gynecologic Structures:
Uterus and bilateral adnexa appear grossly unremarkable given the limitations of non-dedicated pelvic CT. No free pelvic fluid.

Bladder:
Unremarkable. Adequately distended.

---

WHOLE-BODY BONE SCAN (Tc-99m MDP)

Multiple focal areas of increased radiotracer activity were identified on the prior bone scan at the right 5th posterior rib, T10 vertebral body, and right iliac wing. On the current examination:

  - Right 5th posterior rib: Residual mild increased uptake at the known lesion, somewhat diminished in intensity compared to prior study, consistent with post-radiation change and/or healing. Pattern has shifted from intense focal uptake to a milder, more diffuse blush.
  - T10 vertebral body: Focal area of increased uptake at T10 is stable in distribution and intensity compared to prior examination.
  - Right iliac wing: Focal area of increased uptake in the right iliac wing is stable in distribution, without appreciable change in intensity.
  - No new foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake identified throughout the axial or appendicular skeleton. No new sites suspicious for osseous involvement.

Degenerative changes identified at the bilateral knees, lower lumbar facets, and bilateral sacroiliac joints consistent with physiologic findings at the patient's age.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. The two right hepatic lobe metastases have both decreased in size -- the larger lesion down from 1.2 cm to 0.9 cm and the smaller lesion from 0.8 cm to 0.6 cm. No new hepatic lesions. This is consistent with a meaningful response in the liver since initiating olaparib.

2. Previously identified right 5th posterior rib lesion demonstrates sclerotic change around the lytic component and diminished tracer uptake compared to prior, compatible with post-radiation remodeling. No new fracture.

3. T10 vertebral body and right iliac wing osseous lesions are unchanged in size and scintigraphic activity compared to the prior study. The iliac wing lesion measures 1.7 cm, essentially stable.

4. No new pulmonary nodules, pleural effusion, or lymphadenopathy above or below the diaphragm.

5. No new osseous lesions identified on CT or bone scintigraphy.

Overall, the findings reflect encouraging shrinkage of the hepatic metastases alongside stable known osseous disease following approximately three months of olaparib therapy. No evidence of new metastatic sites.
